chunk_summary: Refeeding syndrome risk in children with chronic cardiorespiratory disease requires cautious calorie initiation, electrolyte monitoring, and thiamine supplementation to prevent complications like arrhythmia and respiratory failure.
learning_objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of refeeding syndrome in children with chronic cardiorespiratory disease.
Learn protocols for calorie initiation and electrolyte monitoring in high-risk pediatric patients.
Identify risk factors for refeeding syndrome, including prolonged inadequate intake and significant weight loss.
Apply evidence-based strategies for thiamine supplementation during refeeding.
key_facts: Refeeding syndrome involves insulin-driven shifts of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium into cells, causing complications like arrhythmia and respiratory failure.
Risk factors include negligible intake for >7 days, significant weight loss, very low z-scores, or visible wasting in chronic disease.
Calorie initiation should start at 25% of estimated needs, increasing by 10% daily; in severe cases, start as low as 10 kcal/kg/day.
Electrolytes (phosphate, potassium, magnesium) should be monitored at least daily for the first 48–72 hours, or every 12 hours in high-risk cases.
Thiamine supplementation is recommended before and during refeeding in high-risk children.
reference_quote: Risk identification should be routine when planning rapid catch-up: recent negligible intake for more than about 7 days, significant weight loss, very low z-scores, or chronic disease with visible wasting should trigger protocol-level monitoring. (da Silva et al., 2020)
document_key_topics: Refeeding syndrome risk in chronic cardiorespiratory disease
Calorie initiation protocols for malnourished children
Electrolyte monitoring and supplementation during refeeding
Thiamine supplementation in pediatric nutrition rehabilitation
